evals.name: verb-in-url-trap
evals.prompt: Design REST API endpoints for a user management system. I need endpoints to: create a user, get user details, update user email, delete a user, and activate a suspended user account.
evals.trap: Model uses verbs in URL paths like POST /createUser, GET /getUser/:id, POST /activateUser/:id instead of resource-based nouns with HTTP methods

assertions.text: Uses plural noun /users as the resource — NOT /user, /createUser, /getUser
Uses POST /users for create, GET /users/:id for read, PATCH or PUT for update, DELETE for delete — verbs come from HTTP methods, not URL
Uses a custom action pattern for activate — either POST /users/:id:activate (colon) or POST /users/:id/activate (slash), NOT POST /activateUser/:id
URL paths use slug-case (kebab-case), not camelCase or snake_case

evals.name: singular-vs-plural-resource
evals.prompt: Design API endpoints for a system where each user has exactly one profile (with bio, avatar, settings), and each profile can have multiple shipping addresses. I need to read/update profile, and CRUD for addresses.
evals.trap: Model uses plural /profiles for a singleton resource, or uses singular /address for a collection

assertions.text: Uses singular /users/:id/profile for the profile resource (one profile per user, not a collection)
Uses plural /users/:id/profile/addresses for the addresses collection
GET /users/:id/profile returns the profile directly, not wrapped in an array
Individual address accessed via /users/:id/profile/addresses/:address_id

evals.name: pagination-large-dataset
evals.prompt: I'm building an API for a logging/analytics platform. The events table has 500 million rows. Users need to browse recent events in an infinite scroll UI. Design the list endpoint with pagination.
evals.trap: Model uses page/size or offset/limit pagination which degrades at high offsets and suffers from resource skipping in a high-write table

assertions.text: Uses cursor-based pagination — NOT page/size or offset/limit for a 500M row, high-write dataset
Response includes a cursor or next_cursor field for the client to request the next page
Response includes a has_more or has_next indicator so the client knows when to stop
Explains why offset-based pagination is problematic: performance degradation at high offsets and/or resource skipping when data changes between requests
Cursor is based on a stable, indexed column (e.g., id, timestamp) with consistent ordering

evals.name: idempotency-payment-endpoint
evals.prompt: Design a REST API endpoint for processing payments. A client sends a payment request with debit account, credit account, and amount. Due to network issues, the client might retry the same request. How do you prevent double-charging?
evals.trap: Model designs the endpoint without an idempotency mechanism, or relies solely on client-side deduplication, or returns an error on duplicate instead of the original response

assertions.text: Requires an Idempotency-Key header (or equivalent unique request/transaction ID) on the POST request
Server stores the idempotency key and enforces uniqueness (via DB unique constraint or similar)
On duplicate request, server returns the original successful response — NOT an error or a new transaction
Idempotency key is generated by the client, not the server
Uses POST method for payment creation (not GET or PUT)

evals.name: async-export-large-file
evals.prompt: Design an API for exporting product data as a CSV file. The export can take 5-10 minutes and produce a 500MB file. The client is a web browser.
evals.trap: Model designs a synchronous GET endpoint that blocks until the file is ready, risking request timeout, client blocking, and server memory exhaustion

assertions.text: Uses an async job pattern — initial request returns immediately with a job ID, NOT a synchronous file download
Initial request returns 202 Accepted (not 200) with job reference
Provides a job status polling endpoint (GET /jobs/:id or similar)
Provides a separate endpoint or download URL for retrieving the completed file
Explains why synchronous approach fails: request timeout, client blocking, or memory exhaustion for large files

evals.name: sorting-sql-injection-risk
evals.prompt: Design a product listing API that supports sorting by price, name, or created_at. The client passes the sort field and direction as query parameters. Show the endpoint design and mention any security considerations.
evals.trap: Model passes the sort field directly into ORDER BY clause without validation, creating a SQL injection vector via column name

assertions.text: Specifies a whitelist of allowed sort fields (price, name, created_at) — does NOT pass user input directly to ORDER BY
Defines a consistent sort format in query params (e.g., sort=price:asc or sort=+price)
Has a default sort field and direction when none specified
Mentions SQL injection risk or security concern around user-controlled column names

evals.name: error-response-consistency
evals.prompt: Design the error response format for a REST API used by a payment service. Show how errors should look for: validation failure (missing required field), insufficient balance, unauthorized access, and internal server error.
evals.trap: Model uses inconsistent error formats across different error types — some with message string, some with error object, some with different field names

assertions.text: All error responses use the same envelope structure regardless of error type
Each error has a machine-readable type or code (e.g., INSUFFICIENT_BALANCE, VALIDATION_ERROR) — not just a human message
Each error has a human-readable message field
Uses correct HTTP status codes: 400 for validation, 401 for unauthorized, 500 for internal error — not 200 for all with error in body
Internal server error does NOT leak stack traces, SQL queries, or implementation details to the client

evals.name: versioning-breaking-change
evals.prompt: Our public API currently has a user endpoint that returns age as a string ("25"). We need to change it to an integer (25). This API has 200+ external consumers. How should we handle this change?
evals.trap: Model suggests just changing the field type without versioning, or suggests a migration path that breaks existing consumers

assertions.text: Identifies this as a breaking change that requires versioning (changing field type is not backward compatible)
Recommends creating a new API version (e.g., v2) rather than modifying v1 in place
Suggests a deprecation period where both v1 and v2 coexist so consumers can migrate
Recommends communicating the change and timeline to existing consumers

evals.name: many-to-many-relationship
evals.prompt: Design API endpoints for a school system where students can be enrolled in multiple classes, and each class has multiple students. I need to: list students in a class, add a student to a class, remove a student from a class, and list classes for a student.
evals.trap: Model creates separate endpoints like POST /enrollments with both IDs in body, losing the nested resource relationship in URL structure

assertions.text: Uses nested resource URLs: GET /classes/:class_id/students to list students in a class
Uses POST or PUT /classes/:class_id/students/:student_id to add a student (not just IDs in request body)
Uses DELETE /classes/:class_id/students/:student_id to remove enrollment
Provides reverse lookup: GET /students/:student_id/classes to list classes for a student

evals.name: rate-limit-headers
evals.prompt: Our API is getting hammered by a few clients making thousands of requests per minute. Design a rate limiting strategy and specify how the API should communicate limits to clients.
evals.trap: Model describes rate limiting logic but omits the client-facing headers, or returns a generic 500/403 instead of 429

assertions.text: Returns 429 Too Many Requests when limit is exceeded — NOT 403 or 500
Includes X-RateLimit-Limit header showing the maximum allowed requests
Includes X-RateLimit-Remaining header showing remaining requests in current window
Includes Retry-After or X-RateLimit-Reset header telling the client when to retry

evals.name: request-response-casing
evals.prompt: Design a REST API endpoint to create an order. The order has a customer name, shipping address (street address, zip code, country code), line items with product ID and quantity, and a promo code. Show the full request and response JSON.
evals.trap: Model uses camelCase (shippingAddress, productId, promoCode) in JSON body instead of snake_case, or mixes casing conventions

assertions.text: Request body fields use snake_case: shipping_address, zip_code, country_code, product_id, promo_code
Response body fields also use snake_case consistently
URL path uses slug-case: /order-service/v1/orders or similar (not /orderService or /order_service)
No mixing of camelCase and snake_case within the same request or response

evals.name: api-documentation-completeness
evals.prompt: Write the API documentation for a POST /payment-service/v1/payments endpoint that creates a payment transaction. Include all the sections a developer would need to integrate with this endpoint.
evals.trap: Model writes only the URL and request body, omitting error table, response envelope, headers, or cURL sample

assertions.text: Includes API spec section with method, URL, and required headers (Content-Type, Idempotency-Key)
Includes request body field table with type, required flag, and description for each field
Includes response body with standard envelope structure (meta + data)
Includes error table listing possible error codes, types, and descriptions
Includes a cURL sample for quick testing
